title: Pages & Engines
description: Rendering pages with the Vue or React engine: props, head, stores, SPA

blocks.text: The core has no built-in renderer. Pages render through a pluggable engine you register explicitly — @hushkey/howl-vue (Vue 3 SFCs) or @hushkey/howl-react (React 19 .tsx). Pick one.

Each engine has two halves: the render engine (vueEngine()/reactEngine()) on the Howl constructor, and the esbuild plugin (vuePlugin()/reactPlugin()) on HowlBuilder. The model is thick-client — SSR first paint, then full-page hydration into an SPA. Interactive components are ordinary components in hydrated pages; there is no islands system.
Every page (and layout) receives a typed props object describing the request. Both engines expose the same shape — ReactPageProps<Data, State> and VuePageProps<Data, State>:

rows: The request URL — a real URL on server and client.
Request-scoped ctx.state, serialized into the page.
Data returned by a route handler, if one provided any.

blocks.text: The whole ctx.state is serialized into the page HTML for hydration — treat it as public. Never put secrets on ctx.state; expose only an allowlisted, client-safe subset (e.g. ctx.state.clientContext).
React pages are .tsx files exporting a default component. Use the useHead hook from @hushkey/howl-react/head for per-page <head> tags.
Vue pages are .vue single-file components. Type the props with defineProps<VuePageProps<...>>() and pull useHead from @hushkey/howl-vue/head.

Pages hydrate into a full SPA, so fetch data client-side after first paint — from the typed HTTP client the build generates from apis/ (see Plugins). The server renders the shell for SEO; the client fills in the data.

The generated client is fully typed end-to-end: request bodies, query params and responses are inferred from the Zod schemas in your defineApi definitions, so a contract change surfaces as a type error in the page.
Each engine re-exports its ecosystem's store library, wired into hydration so server state survives the SSR → client handoff.

For emails, notifications, or any markup produced outside the page/layout flow, ctx.renderToString() renders a standalone component to an HTML string with the registered engine — no layouts, app shell, or headers.

const html = await ctx.renderToString(WelcomeEmail, { name: user.name });
await mailer.send({ to: user.email, html });

When there is no ctx — a cron job, a queue worker, a script — import renderToString from the engine directly. The React engine's is the drop-in for Preact's render from preact-render-to-string, and accepts either a JSX element or a component plus props.
